planting.note: Self-sows freely and typically requires no direct sowing once established in a moist, shaded location; plants emerge from the previous year's seed bank in late spring. In new locations, direct sow in moist soil in shade after last frost and keep evenly moist until germination.

directions: Allow to naturalize in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; pale jewelweed is a native annual of moist, shaded forest floors, streambanks, and seepy woodland edges across eastern North America. Closely related to the orange-flowered spotted jewelweed (I. capensis), this species produces clear, pale yellow flowers with a more open, cup-like shape and faint reddish-brown spotting. The explosive seed pods are famously touch-sensitive — ripe pods rupture with a forceful spring mechanism when touched, flinging seeds several feet — a feature particularly delightful for children. The succulent, translucent stems produce a watery sap long used to soothe poison ivy rash and insect stings. An excellent self-sowing shade annual that naturalizes rapidly in moist woodland gardens, forming showy drifts of yellow from midsummer through fall. Hummingbirds and bumblebees pollinate the flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or divide established clumps in early spring. Halberd-leaved yellow violet grows naturally in rich, well-drained mesic woodland slopes, especially in association with oak-hickory and cove hardwood forests.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade on wooded slopes and in cove hardwood forests; halberd-leaved yellow violet is one of the more distinctive native woodland violets, named for the unusual shape of its leaves — triangular with two spreading basal lobes that resemble the blade of a medieval halberd. The foliage often displays attractive silver mottling along the veins, adding interest beyond the spring blooming period. Clear lemon-yellow flowers with purple veining appear in early spring, providing one of the earliest woodland floral displays. Unlike many forest violets, this species prefers well-drained, sloped woodland sites rather than moist bottomland conditions. An excellent groundcover for dry, shaded slopes in naturalistic woodland gardens where its spreading colonies create attractive spring ground-level interest before the tree canopy closes in.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring just as growth emerges; direct seed in fall for germination 1–2 years later after extended cold stratification. Perfoliate bellwort grows naturally in rich, well-drained, acidic to neutral woodland soils under deciduous forest.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; perfoliate bellwort is a delicate native woodland perennial, distinguished from its close relative large-flowered bellwort (U. grandiflora) by having stems that appear to pierce through the center of the clasping leaves — giving the characteristic 'perfoliate' appearance that names the species. The nodding, pale yellow, tubular flowers on arching stems in spring have a graceful pendant quality and orange-granular texture on the inner surfaces. The plant grows naturally in rich, well-drained deciduous woodland soils from the Gulf Coast to Canada, making it suitable for zone 9 woodland gardens with good drainage and consistent summer moisture. An excellent spring-blooming companion for trilliums, wild ginger, and mayapple in the native woodland garden. Goes dormant by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant small bulbils in spring in average to well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; pink wood sorrel spreads readily by bulbils and self-sown seed, naturalizing rapidly in disturbed areas, lawn edges, and garden beds throughout the Gulf South.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in zones 8–11; pink wood sorrel is a South American species widely naturalized throughout the Gulf South and subtropical regions, where it is familiar as a low, spreading perennial of lawns, garden edges, and disturbed ground. The characteristic trifoliate clover-like leaves fold up at night and reopen each morning in a consistent daily rhythm. The bright rose-pink flowers with darker veining are produced profusely from fall through spring in zone 9 — the opposite of most temperate plants — making it a valuable cool-season color plant that performs when annuals have been killed by frost. The small underground bulbils spread the plant readily, so it can become persistent in garden beds; it is best managed as a groundcover in areas where its spreading habit is acceptable. The leaves are edible with a pleasant, mild lemon-sorrel flavor from oxalic acid.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring in cool, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; brook saxifrage grows naturally along shaded, cool mountain stream banks and spray zones of waterfalls in the southern Appalachians, requiring consistent moisture and cool root temperatures.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, cool, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade alongside streams, water features, and seepy areas; brook saxifrage is an Appalachian endemic of shaded mountain stream banks, spray zones, and seepage areas in the southern Appalachian Mountains, where it grows in constantly moist, cool, shaded conditions near running water. The deeply palmately lobed, maplelike leaves resemble small monkshood (Aconitum) leaves — giving the species its epithet — and are attractive throughout the growing season. Loose panicles of small white flowers rise above the foliage in early summer. Requires consistent soil moisture and will not tolerate drought or heat; in zone 7 and warmer, site in the deepest, coolest shade available with consistent moisture from a water feature or seep. Excellent for naturalistic stream-side or water garden plantings in mountain gardens and cool-climate shade gardens.
